speaker: interviewer
text: Slate number six. Today's date is March 27, 1996. We're conducting an interview with a survivor, Mr. Marvin Zborowski. Mr. Zborowski, would you please introduce your family through your family photographs?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is Great-Grandfather, being taken at the turn of the century. And the name is Moshe Dauman, being called the [HEBREW] Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Zarki, Poland. Significant about the picture is that he is-- he is wearing a [? galut ?] hat, which was a typical hat in that part of Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my grandfather and grandmother on my maternal side. Their names are-- my grandmother, Ester Dauman and Samuel Dauman. They were killed in January 1943, at the railroad track.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radomsko, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my father, Moshe Zborowski, and my mother, Zisel Zborowski. My father was killed in July, August 1943 by the Poles. My mother died in 1963 in the United States of America. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It isn't-- One second. May I just say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1923, when their-- was their engagement. My father was killed in 1943, and my mother died in 1963. This picture was taken in the early '20s, when he was attending the yeshiva high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Moshe Zborowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Zarki, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This picture was taken in July, 1935, at the time that one of our relatives went to Canada. Sitting on the-- Sitting from the left side, my brother Eli, my sister, [? Cesia ?] myself, Cousin Helen, and Uncle Moshe. The second row, two of my aunts, Sala and Rachel. And the third line from left to right, my late mother, my aunt [? Cesia ?] my Uncle Sam, cousin which left for Canada, and my Aunt Bella. And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Zisel Zborowski.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken during our time we went to a spa, and I am with my parents, and it was taken in August, 1939. My- My mother, Zisel on the left, myself in center, and my late father in [? Streletzia, ?] Poland. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] This picture was taken during the war. And significant about it-- about the David star of my aunt wearing or holding it. From my left sitting, my Cousin Helen survived, my aunt [? Salcha, ?] killed, and my sister [? Cesia ?], survived. This picture was given to me by my uncle, which survived, and this picture was taken in 1942, where it shows a group of Jews with their gear being taken to clean the roads, which was so vital for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zarki, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is very significant and has a great meaning to me. This was at the time taken in 1941, 1942, when no Jewish children were allowed to go to school. By the-- by the graciousness of the teacher, which risked her life, which is at the extreme right, sitting-- her name was Judith, coming from the town of Plotzk-- giving, in hiding, so to say, courses in-- in elementary school. I will try to recall the names. From my left, a little boy by the name Baum last name. The girl, next, is Varshawski. The next one, Granek Next one, which I was very fond, and I would say that- consider, today's date, as a girlfriend, Zoshia Nioman. The teacher, Judith. Going back again from left, myself, standing, my friend Steinbrecher killed, and the last name, I am sorry. I do not remember the name anymore. I know that he had frostbites, when he came back from hiding somewhere, but he got killed. None of those survived. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Zarki in 1941. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nothing more to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say 13. I didn't speak about my great bar mitzvah. I should actually speak about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us something now about your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being that this picture is being taken during the time that I was already a bar mitzvah boy, I did not have any bar mitzvah, any big parties. Don't ask me how many people attended my party. It was simply-- the day that-- that was my day of bar mitzvah, I was told that I am 13. I was given a pair of tefillin, which is a- and they said to me, put it on the left hand. And they put this on your hat. The next day, I did not remember which one I put on this side, and it fell off. It was very-- I was-- I was not aware. So I hope that, one day, reaching at the age of 83, to celebrate my second bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: With God's help. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken right after the liberation in 1945. Before speaking and naming the names, I would like to start with the most outstanding one, the little baby there. This baby was saved through the monastery and orphanage, and we rescued her after the war. Now, starting from-- from this-- from left to the right, sitting position, my aunt [? Cesia ?] the baby, and her husband, my Uncle Sam Jonisch. Next, my mother, Zisel Zborowski. Standing from left to right, myself, my sister [? Cesia ?] my Uncle Joseph, my Aunt Rachel, and my brother, Eli. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture is taken in Zarki at the bunker place in the cellar, which I described before. From the picture, you could see, it's my sister and myself. The significance of it is that it shows you the bricks and the deep in cut that you could see. That wall in the back of us is the one that was sliding and movable. This picture was taken in 1983, when the Polish-Israeli relationship was not as great as today, whereby, at the city hall in Warsaw, a-- an official presentation is being made to the daughter, Mrs.- Miss [? Yadviga ?] Placzek And standing from left to right, making the presentation, are the three survivors from her parents-- from her parents. Eli, my brother, [? Cesia ?] my sister, the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Placzek what helped us survive, and myself. This picture is of Mr. Jozef Kolacz. That's the peasant which we were in hiding, and he rescued 10 Jews plus two Jews at his sister's house, for a total of 12 Jews. Mr. Kolacz came to Israel in 1987, whereby he was getting the official recognition and planted a tree on the Alley of the Righteous. His name is Jozef Kolacz and myself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the wedding picture of-- on our wedding day, June 20th, 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, we got married in the part of New York called the Bronx, and the place was the Parkside Plaza. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your bride's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name, Celina Sternlicht now married, Zborowski. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1987, and at the place of Kielce, Poland, where-- at the time of restoration of the Jewish cemetery. Kielce has a special meaning to the Polish Jews and to the world Jewry, especially at the pogrom, which was taking place in June, 1946, where 50 or 60 Jews were killed, merciless.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Poles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture was taken at one of the Yad Vashem annual dinners. From left to right is minister Arik Sharon, myself, and my brother, Eli Zborowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Minister of which country-- in which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arik Sharon? Minister in Israel. He had various portfolios. This picture was taken in 1992 at Yad Vashem. At the time, I was presented of being on the Board of Yad Vashem Museum in Jerusalem. The presentation was made by the honorary chairman, Secretary Joseph [? Bourke. ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in the late '80s with the former governor, Mario Cuomo, which I consider a good friend, and at that time, a neighbor, as well. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the wedding picture of our youngest son, Ziggy, to [? Galete, ?] which was on August-- I'm sorry, it was on March 15, 1992. From left to right, you could see, sitting, my daughter-in-law, married to my older son, Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judy. Judy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next, Ziggy. Next, [? Galete. ?] And my wife, sitting. Standing, my older son, Mark, with lovely baby Matthew, and myself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Matthew, my grandson, age five, the son of Judith and Mark Zborowski. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my princess, Laura, the daughter of Judith and Mark Zborowski, age two and a half. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my grandson Jeremy, the son of [? Galete ?] and Ziggy Zborowski, age two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Zborowski, we would like to thank you very much for sharing your family album with us. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Zborowski, would you please introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, gladly. To my right, my wife. In the back of me is my older son, Mark, and to his right is my younger son, Ziggy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Zborowski, since both you and your husband are survivors, and have gone through Shoah experiences, as you look back, do you think it was easier to being-- being married to someone who had an understanding of what you went through, and you understood what your husband went through? In other words, shared experiences, did it make it easier for you to-- to bear the burden of the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. It was definitely easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss the Shoah amongst you often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, practically every Shabbos at the table, it was my  husband's thought that he was telling us his stories. The children were listening, and even my daughter-in-laws, they said, so what is the topic today? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I would ask Mark, growing up in the house with parents who were survivors, did you find, when speaking to your friends who did not have these experiences, did you find that you were more sensitive, brought up in a different way, or that you looked at life differently? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a-- it's an often-asked question, from a myriad of reasons-- for a myriad of reasons. But I think that growing up as a child of a survivor, you definitely do have-- in my experience, anyhow-- there's more of a sense of family, family being the be all, end all. Everything else is-- is a clear second to-- to life, as opposed to the life-- the growing up, the childhood of friends of ours that were not children of survivors. And therefore, I-- I-- I definitely believe that growing up as a second generation, first generation survivor was considerably different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did you do any reading in the Shoah materials, in the memoirs? Do you do any reading?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, like my father-- I'm a voracious reader. I don't think there are many publications that have been written that I have not read. Especially poignant is an article that I read yesterday in about three new books that are actually coming out that said that the Germans who have, for all these years, said it was not the Germans, it was the Nazis. We did not know. I find it especially telling that, in yesterday's there was an article about this very book that is coming out that is discussing that the Germans clearly did know, and they did collaborate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ziggy, you now have a child of your own. How do you think that being a son of survivors is going to influence upbringing of your own child, your own family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Growing up as a child of not only one survivor, but having both parents being survivors, family was always stressed as the utmost key. And the relationship that I have with my parents, as well as my brother has grown even tighter as we've grown up, and it only just will keep multiplying by sharing the joys of my son, or my children, or my sons to my parents. And explaining to them where they came from, and how we're so close, that we hope it'll have a rippling effect through our family, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother, Mark, just mentioned the deniers, in a certain way-- different deniers-- but anyway, what- how do you think, or do you think, at all, about carrying on the torch to keep remembering the Kedoshim and- and perpetuating, actually, the remembrance and remembering of- of those who have been martyred, and of those who are still alive and are survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that the only way-- I mean, the Yom HaShoa, the remembrance and the survivors were just as important as anything that happened in the Jewish religion years prior to my existence. And the same way with the World War II, that this existed before I was. This will be remembered as a piece of history and Jewish tradition, and they will never be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your parents' experiences, looking at it from as children's point of views, being brought up by survivors, do you think that your parents were able to integrate into the society well enough, even with the scars that they are carrying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. They made it a point that they wanted our-- or their children, meaning us, my brother and I, to have a better life than they had. And growing up over the years, as we were little kids, we were often always compared to our age as where they were during that time. For example, when I was 12, or my brother was 16, those were the years that my father was going through the Holocaust. So anything that we were privileged enough to get, it was always brought back. Not as something for credit, but just like, when I was your age, I didn't have a bicycle, or a toy, or a car, or whatever it was, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, what is, actually, your hope or your dream for the future, in light of the fact of the world, the way the world is right now, and all still-- all the things that are going on? What is your hope for the future for the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peace would very nice, but just to expand on that note, I think that the world should not forget the lessons of the fact that history, if we do forget, it will repeat itself. Unfortunately, the crisis in Israel right now, much to the regret, I guess, of all the peace participants, seems to have stagnated at present, and I think it is very important that we, as children of survivors, survivors, and human mankind do not forget what happened, what can happen. You know, at one point, you know, Hitler was just one raving madman. There are many of these so-called raving mad men in the world today, and like I earlier said, like I said earlier, we should not forget what could happen if we forget our past.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Zborowski, in conclusion, would you like to add anything else, any thoughts about the Shoah or about your family, and speak to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to stress, once again, the importance-- and being that we're approaching now, in a few weeks, the day of the Yom HaShoah and many other commemorations. It's even my own lecture is being, now, scheduled for April 17, 1996 in Queens College, of the endowment that Celina and Marvin Zborowski made to the Queens College. This should be stressed, and it should be done, and encouraged to people of all races, of all beliefs, that this should not happen again. Now, in conclusion, now, to my future generation, to my own children, I'm taking great pride that they are with us. They lived through the same thing. It's not easy, many times, to be children of survivors, and we have many shortcomings. But I would definitely say they have everything they could ask for. And I'm pleased that both my children, both my sons, and I hope that their sons will do, and their children will perpetuate the same way, and be as nice and charitable as they are now. My both sons are involved in going in the footstep of their forefathers, meaning myself and my own fathers and grandparents, of being kind and charitable in every endeavor. And this gives me great pride. Of course, their success is my success, but the investment of giving, of being kind, is the best that I could enjoy, and I hope I will be able to enjoy for many years to come. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for introducing your family to us and for sharing your experiences with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
